coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots incorporated in hierarchical macro/mesoporous g-C3N4/TiO2 as an all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction for enhancement of photocatalytic H2 evolution under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Zifei
Shi, Dan
Wang, Guohong
Gao, Tengyuan
Wang, Juan
Lu, Lianying
Li, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, coupling TiO2 with other narrow bandgap semiconductors to improve photocatalytic hydrogen generation rate has attracted widespread attention. Herein, carbon dots (C dots) decorated g-C3N4/TiO2 all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts with hierarchical macro/mesoporous structure have been fabricated via a facile solvothermal and calcination method. The dosage of C dots greatly influences the microstructure and the visible-light photocatalytic H2 evolution rate (HER). Under the optimal condition, the ternary composites consisting of C dots, g-C3N4 and TiO2 exhibited the highest HER of 580 μmol g−1 h−1, exceed the pure TiO2, pure g-C3N4 and the binary g-C3N4/TiO2 hybrid photocatalyst by a fact of 6.6 times, 2.1 times and 1.6 times, respectively. The excellent photocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution is ascribed to the stronger light absorption, higher specific surface area and faster charge transportation. An all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism was proposed in which C dots serve as a mediator to improve the separation and transportation of photo-excited charge carriers.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the fabrication of CCTx composites.

SEM images of the pure TiO2 macro/mesoporous framework (a and b), (c) and (d) CCT0.05 ternary hybrid catalyst. (a) and (c) show the external microcahnnels surface of TiO2 and CCT0.05. (b) and (d) show the internal surface of cleaved TiO2 and CCT0.05. (e) TEM and HRTEM (f) images of the CCT0.05 ternary hybrid catalyst. (g) energy-dispersive X-ray mapping images of CCT0.05.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of the various samples.

(a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of pure C dots (the inset in panel a), TiO2, g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05 photocatalysts, and (b) the corresponding pore size distribution curves of corresponding samples.
XPS spectra of (a) the survey spectra, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) Ti 2p and (e) O1s of the corresponding samples.

(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the C dots, TiO2, g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05 powders, (b) Mott-Schottky plots collected for g-C3N4 (the inset) and TiO2 at a frequency of 1000 Hz in the dark condition in 0.5 M Na2SO4 (pH = 7) vs Ag/AgCl reference electrode (filled with saturated KCl), (c) PL spectra of g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05 powders under 370 nm excitation, and (d) time-resolved fluorescence emission spectra of g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05 under the illumination of a 375 nm laser.

(a) Comparison of the H2 evolution rate of different photocatalysts under visible light irradiation with four LED lamps (420 nm, 3 W), (b) cycling runs for the photocatalytic H2 evolution of CCT0.05 photocatalyst, and (c) the FT-IR spectra of the CCT0.05 photocatalyst before and after cycling tests.

(a) Photocurrent responses in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution (pH = 7) with 1.5 V bias versus the Ag/AgCl reference electrode and (b) the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of g-C3N4, TiO2, CT, and CCT-0.05 electrodes.

Schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism of the ternary CCT0.05 catalyst for hydrogen production under visible light irradiation due to the formation of an all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction based on C dots as a direct electron/hole mediator between the TiO2 macro-/meso-porous framework and g-C3N4 particles. Thus all redox potentials are converted to those relative to NHE.

(a) The time-dependent PL spectra of hydroxyl terephthalic acid during LED light irradiation (420 nm) of the CCT0.05 sample in the 100 mL aqueous solution with 2 mM NaOH and 0.5 mM terephthalic acid (pH = 11). (b) Comparison of PL intensity versus the irradiation time for the TiO2, g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05 samples. All PL measurements were done with an excitation wavelength of 315 nm.
Effects of C dot content on the physical properties of CCTx samples.
List of the averaged lifetime of various samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots incorporated in hierarchical macro/mesoporous g-C3N4/TiO2 as an all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction for enhancement of photocatalytic H2 evolution under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, coupling TiO2 with other narrow bandgap semiconductors to improve photocatalytic hydrogen generation rate has attracted widespread attention. Herein, carbon dots (C dots) decorated g-C3N4/TiO2 all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts with hierarchical macro/mesoporous structure have been fabricated via a facile solvothermal and calcination method. The dosage of C dots greatly influences the microstructure and the visible-light photocatalytic H2 evolution rate (HER). Under the optimal condition, the ternary composites consisting of C dots, g-C3N4 and TiO2 exhibited the highest HER of 580 μmol g−1 h−1, exceed the pure TiO2, pure g-C3N4 and the binary g-C3N4/TiO2 hybrid photocatalyst by a fact of 6.6 times, 2.1 times and 1.6 times, respectively. The excellent photocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution is ascribed to the stronger light absorption, higher specific surface area and faster charge transportation. An all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism was proposed in which C dots serve as a mediator to improve the separation and transportation of photo-excited charge carriers.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis using semiconductor nanomaterials have attracted intense interests in recent years due to its potential as a sustainable technology for removing organic pollutants or toxic gases and producing hydrogen or oxygen by splitting water using natural sunlight [1–4]. TiO2 has been one of the most widely studied semiconductor photocatalysts due to its low cost, outstanding stability, and availability in various structures and morphologies [5–8]. Extensive attentions have been paid to further enhance the photocatalytic properties of TiO2 by overcoming its intrinsic limits such as wide bandgap (3.2 eV) and fast recombination of photo-generated charge carriers. One of the attractive approaches is to develop heterojunction materials by incorporating semiconductors with narrower bandgaps in order to expand photon absorption into visible light and separate photo-excited electrons and holes across the interface [9–11]. While two semiconductors with staggered band structures are in good contact, they tend to form traditional type II heterojunctions, in which the photo-excited electrons in the junction interface will flow from one conduction band (CB) with higher energy into the another CB with lower energy, while the holes flow in the valence band (VB) in the opposite direction. This effectively separates the photo-induced electrons and holes in the interface of two different materials so that they have more chance to react with the target molecules instead of quickly recombine [12]. However, the disadvantage of the traditional type II heterojunction is that the redox ability of photo-generated electrons and holes is weakened owing to transferring into the less energetic states. Hence, it is difficult to possess both the high charge-separation efficiency and strong redox capacity in the traditional type II heterojunction simultaneously. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a new-type photocatalytic system to overcome the aforementioned problems.

By properly engineering the interface, a Z-scheme heterojunction can be formed between two semiconductors where photo-generated electrons flow from the lower CB into the VB of the other semiconductor and recombine with the photo-excited holes. Meanwhile, the photo-excited electrons in the higher CB and the photo-excited holes in the lower VB are retained in separate semiconductors. This has the advantages in retaining the higher reducing power of electrons in the semiconductor with a higher conduction band edge (CBE) and the higher oxidizing power of holes in the other semiconductor with a lower valence band edge (VBE). Typically, there are three mechanisms to form Z-scheme photocatalysts. Firstly, electron acceptor (A) or donor (D) pairs in solution (such as Fe3+/Fe2+ and IO3 − /I−) are used as redox mediators to shuttle electron between the two semiconductors [13]. This is very similar to the Z-scheme in the natural photosynthesis systems. Secondly, a direct Z-scheme semiconductor interface is constructed to facilitate the electron/hole transfer. This has been demonstrated by many researchers including our own group in the binary heterojunction systems. For example, we have found that the C-/N- doping at the shallow surface of a TiO2 with macro/mesoporous framework helps to form a Z-scheme heterojunction between g-C3N4 and TiO2, which greatly enhances the photocatalytic degradation of Rohdamine B with visible light [14]. Thirdly, conductive materials (such as Cu, Ag, and Au nanoparticles) have been used as perfect electron mediators to construct all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalysts [15]. Carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon dots (C dots), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene [16–21], have also been demonstrated as effective electron mediators for Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts. Particularly, C dots can serve as both acceptor and donor of electrons, which boost the electron transfer efficiency [22–25]. In addition, C dots are low-cost, environmentally friendly and stable [26–31].

In this study we extend our previous success in the binary Z-scheme g-C3N4/TiO2 photocatalyst by incorporating different amount of C dots. We demonstrate that proper C dots loading produces a ternary all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction with C dots as the electron/hole mediator. About 66% higher hydrogen production rate has been obtained under visible-light irradiation than the corresponding binary Z-scheme g-C3N4/TiO2 photocatalyst. The C-dots have been found to play three roles in the C Dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 ternary system: (1) serving as excellent electron mediators [32], and (2) increase the total specific surface area (SBET) of the catalyst [33–34]. This opens a new way to optimize the heterojunction photocatalysts [35].

Tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT), triethanolamine, 25% ammonia, and melamine were supplied by Tianjin Komiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.. All chemicals were employed without further treatments. Deionized (DI) water was used in the experiments.

The C dots were fabricated by combining simple burning and hydrothermal treatment method. Firstly, the candle soot was collected by placing a glass plate above the flame of a burning candle. Secondly, the candle soot was dissolved in 50 mL ammonia solution and ultra-sonicated for 30 min. Then, the suspension was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave with a capacity of 100 mL and heated at 150 °C for 7 h [32]. After hydrothermal treatment, the black precipitate was centrifuged and washed with DI water and ethanol three times, respectively. Finally, the product was dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h. The as-synthesized C dots have spherical shapes with an average size of 17 nm in diameter as shown in Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Information. Interestingly, the C dots present a weak up-conversion photoluminescence emission in the range of 280–400 nm (peaked at ∼300 nm) under an excition wavelength of 530 nm (see Fig. S2 in the Supplementary Information).

Firstly, 20 mL of tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT) was added into 200 mL deionized water dropwise, and then aged for 36 h [36]. Secondly, the precipitates were thoroughly washed with ethanol and water for five times, respectively. Finally, the white precipitates were dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain the macro/mesoporous TiO2 powders.

The combination of soaking and thermal annealing was used to fabricate the C dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 hybrids. Typically, 3 g melamine was added in 100 mL deionized water with heating up to 100 °C until it was completely dissolved. Then, 0.01 g C dots and 0.5 g macro/mesoporous TiO2 powder were added into the above melamine solution and ultra-sonicated for 30 min, and then dehydrated in an oven at 80 °C overnight. Subsequently, the dried powders were heated at 550 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace with a ramping rate of 5 °C min−1 under air atmosphere. After cooling, the black-colored C dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 samples were obtained. In this manner, the ternary C dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 composites with different loadings of C dots were prepared and denoted as CCTx, where x (x = 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1 and 0.2 g) represents the mass of the C dots added into the 0.5 g of TiO2 powder in the above procedure. The binary g-C3N4/TiO2 sample in absence of C dots was named as CT. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) in Fig. S3 in the Supplementary Information reveals that the composition of the ternary CCT0.05 sample contains 58.6% g-C3N4, 3.3% C dots and 38.1% TiO2. The schematic diagram of the procedure to prepare the CCTx all-solid-state heterojunctions is shown in Fig. 1 .

The crystallographic structure of the as-prepared samples was characterized by Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German) using Cu-Kα1 radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å, 40 kV/20 mA) in a 2θ range of 10-80° with a step size of 0.02° s−1. The fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of the as-prepared samples were tested from 400 to 4000 cm−1 using the Niconet 5700 spectrometer (Niconet, USA). The microstructure of the samples was characterized by the scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-3400N, Hitachi, Japan) and the transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G20, FEI, USA). The BET specific surface area was evaluated on a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 HD 88 nitrogen adsorption apparatus (Micromeritics, USA). TGA was done by using a TGA-7 thermal gravimetric analyzer (Perkin Elmer, USA). The diffuse reflectance absorption spectra (DRS) of the solid sample were recorded with a UV2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan.) using BaSO4 as the reference. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a Perkin Elmer LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer LS55, Germany) in the range from 200 to 800 nm. The photoelectrochemical measurements were carried out with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E, China) in a typical three-electrode setup with a Pt sheet counter electrode and a Ag/AgCl reference electrode (filled with saturated KCL). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on Al-Kα (1486.68 eV) radiation (VG ESCALAB 220i-XL, UK). The time-resolved transient PL decay curves were performed on a fluorescence lifetime spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FLSP920, UK) under 370 nm excitation.

A photocatalytic hydrogen production test was conducted in a 100 mL three-neck flask. Triethanolamine and H2PtCl6 were added as the sacrificial agent and cocatalyst, respectively. Typically, 50 mg of as-prepared photocatalysts were dispersed in 80 mL triethanolamine aqueous solution (10 vol%). Then, 34 μL H2PtCl6 aqueous solution was injected into the above suspension under stirring (pH = 11) [37–39]. The three-neck flask was sealed with rubber stoppers. Before illumination, the suspension was purged by N2 for 30 min to remove the dissolved oxygen. Four low-power LED lamps (3 W, 420 nm) were employed as the irradiation source. The irradiation area and effective intensity of each LED were 1 cm2 and ca 80 mW/cm2, respectively. About 1 mL of the evolved gas was sampled in every one hour to analyze the hydrogen content using a gas chromatography with a thermal conductivity detector (SP-7280, China). In the cycling experiments, the catalyst mixed solution was reused to repeat the above procedures three times [40].

The photoelectrochemical properties (such as the photocurrents and electrochemical impedance) of the as-prepared samples were performed using a CHI 660C electrochemical analyzer with a three-electrode setup. Typically, 10 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in 2.5 mL ethanol to form a uniform suspension. Subsequently, 0.15 mL of the above suspension was added into 0.15 mL of 1 vol% Nafion D-520 alcohol dispersion and ultra-sonicated for 15 min. Then, the above mixture (0.3 mL) was spread onto an ITO slide with a surface area of ca. 1.0 cm2. After dried in oven at 100 °C for 15 min, the ITO slide with the photocatalyst was used as the working electrode. Meanwhile, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode (filled with saturated KCl solution) and a Pt sheet counter electrode were used, with 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte solution (pH = 7). A 3 W 420 nm LED lamp was used as the light source.

Fig. 2 a and b present the SEM images of the as-prepared TiO2 powder. Similar to our previous reports [36,41], the TiO2 material forms parallel microchannels with the diameter around 3 to 4 μm. A small amount of TiO2 nanoparticles (a few hundreds of nanometers in size) are decorated onto the inner surface of the TiO2 microchannels. In CCT0.05 samples used in the present study, the larger g-C3N4 nanoparticles (50 to ∼1000 nm) and the smaller C dots further fill the microchannels of the TiO2 framework as shown in Fig. 2c and 2d. The TEM image in Fig. 2e reveals that the TiO2 nanoparticles, larger g-C3N4 nanoparticles and much smaller C dots are well mixed in the CCT0.05 sample. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.34 nm in HRTEM image in Fig. 2f can be indexed to the (1 0 1) planes of anatase TiO2 while the 0.32 nm lattice fringes are attributed to C-dots [42]. More TEM images can be found in Fig. S4 (in the Supplementary Information) to show the morphologies and crystal lattices of the CCT0.05 ternary catalyst. The EDS mapping images of CCT0.05 (Fig. 2g) indicate that the sample containes Ti, O, C, and N elements, suggesting the coexistence of g-C3N4 and TiO2.

The XRD patterns of C dots, TiO2, g-C3N4, g-C3N4/TiO2 and CCTx samples were given in Fig. 3 a. It is shown that the C dots show a peak at 25.6°, which is related to the (0 0 1) diffraction peak of the stacked graphene layers with a interlayer d spacing of d = 3.48 Å [16]. As for pure C3N4, two pronounced peaks at 13.1° and 27.3° can be assigned to the (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) planes of graphitic carbon nitride (JCPDS No. 87-1526) [43,44], which match well with the in-plane packing of tri-s-triazine motifs (with the lattice a = 6.76 Å) and interlayer stacking of conjugated aromatic systems (with d = 3.27 Å), respectively. The pure TiO2 presents three distinct peaks at 2θ = 25.3°, 37.8° and 48.1°, corresponding to (1 0 1), (0 0 4) and (2 0 0) crystal planes of the anatase phase, respectively (JCPDS No. 21-1272). Obviously, the binary g-C3N4/TiO2 composite (named as CT) possesses the characteristic diffraction peaks of both g-C3N4 and TiO2. Interestingly, after introducing C dots, the ternary CCT0.01 sample shows XRD peaks almost at the same positions as that of the CT sample. With the increase of C dots loading, no obvious changes were found in the peak positions but the ratio of the peak intensity of g-C3N4 to that of TiO2 increased. This implies that the introduction of C dots does not affect the crystal structure of TiO2 and g-C3N4 but somehow promotes the formation of more g-C3N4 crystals. As shown in Fig. S3, the C dot loading is only 3.3% in the CCT0.05 sample. Thus no C dots can be observed by XRD in CCTx samples.

As shown in Fig. 3b, the FT-IR spectra are recorded to further analyze the functional groups in the C dots, g-C3N4, TiO2, CT and CCT0.05 samples. For the C dots, the three main peaks located at 1046, 1399 and 1628 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of C-O-C, –COO and C = C/C-O, respectively [17]. For pristine g-C3N4, the intense absorption bands located at 1239, 1357, 1408, 1524, and 1571 cm−1 are related to the characteristic stretching vibration modes of C-N and C = N heterocycles, respectively. Meanwhile, the sharp band near 805 cm−1 is attributed to the particular stretching vibration mode of tri-s-triazine ring units. The broad peak between 3000 and 3300 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of terminal NH2 or NH groups of g-C3N4 and stretching vibration of O–H. While, for the pure TiO2, the absorption peaks ranging from 400 to 700 cm−1 can be attributed to Ti-O stretching and Ti-O-Ti bridge stretching modes of TiO2. Besides, the other two peaks located around 1650 and 3400–3500 cm−1 can be assigned to O-H bending mode and the absorption of O-H stretching vibration, respectively [45,46]. For the CT and CCT0.05 samples, the primary peaks of g-C3N4 and TiO2 are observed, verifying the existence of both g-C3N4 and TiO2. However, the characteristic peaks of C dots cannot be observed in the CCT0.05 sample owing to the low content.

As shown in the Fig. 4 a, type IV isotherms can be found in all samples. Further observation shows that the C dots (see the inset of Fig. 4a) and g-C3N4 have H3 hysteresis loops, which are usually found in the catalysts with slit-shaped pores. This suggests the presence of macropores in the C dots and g-C3N4 samples. The pure TiO2 sample presents a H2 hysteresis loop, indicating the existence of mesopores. However, it is notable that the CT and CCT0.05 composites have distinct hysteresis loops (a combination of H2 and H3 types) against the pure C dots, g-C3N4 and TiO2 samples. In the relative pressure range of 0.4–0.8, the type H2 loop corresponds to the network pore structure with ink-bottle shape resulted from the relative large g-C3N4 nanoparticles stuffed within the bulk TiO2 micro-channels [14]. In the high relative pressure range of 0.9–1.0, the type H3 hysteresis loop is related to narrow slit-shape mesopores between interconnected bulk materials [47]. Moreover, the equivalent Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distributions for the five samples are plotted in Fig. 4b. The peaks are located at 33.3, 15.6 and 31.5 nm for C dots, TiO2 and g-C3N4 samples, respectively. While the average pore diameter of the binary CT composite was centered at 11.5 nm. The narrow pore diameter may be resulted from the aggregation of TiO2 nanoparticles. Interestingly, the average pore sizes of CCTx samples with lower C dot dosage (x = 0.01 and 0.02) is much smaller than that of the binary CT composite, while under the higher C dot contents, the CCTx samples (x = 0.05, 0.1 and 0.2) possess the bigger pore sizes from 11.5 to 16.7 nm. These results further confirm that the C dots can tune the microstructure of the ternary CCTx composites [48].

The BET specific surface areas (S BET), pore volume and pore diameter distribution of as-prepared samples is demonstrated in Table 1 . Obviously, the C dots exhibit the highest specific surface areas of 680.8 m2·g−1. On the contrary, the g-C3N4 sample has the lowest the S BET of 8.2 m2·g−1. Interestingly, the S BET of the CT composite is higher than those of the pure g-C3N4 and TiO2. This may be due to the fact that the g-C3N4 powders calcined by melamine were well distributed on the walls of macro/mesoporous TiO2. It is noteworthy that after introducing C dots, the ternary CCTx composites display higher surface areas than the binary CT sample. Further observation indicates that the S BET of the CCTx samples monotonically increases with the C dot loading. This is due to the much larger specific surface area of the C dots.

XPS analysis is usually used to acquire information about the surface chemical composition and chemical states of elements in the as-prepared samples. Fig. 5 a presents the full survey spectra of C dots, g-C3N4, TiO2, CT and CCT-0.05 samples. For pure carbon dots, the stronger peak located at 284.8 eV corresponds to the sp2 C-C bonds of graphitic carbon. For pristine TiO2, the signals of the Ti, O and C elements are detected. The existence of carbon is due to the residual carbon rather than intrinsic composition in the pure TiO2 sample. The typical peaks of the C, N and O species are found in the pure g-C3N4 sample, while the weak O 1s peak arises from the adsorbed oxygen molecule. For the CT and CCT 0.05 composites, all four typical signals of C, N, Ti, and O are detected in the survey spectra.

Fig. 5b displays the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra of the C dots, TiO2, g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05 samples. The above five samples have a common peak at the same binding energies at 284.8 eV, which corresponds to graphitic carbon or residual carbon. Nevertheless, in case of g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05, an additional peak appears at ca. 288 eV, which can be ascribed to the carbon with N-C = N2 conjugated structure. Further observation indicates that, comparing to the pure g-C3N4, the peaks in CT and CCT0.05 samples shift by 0.2 eV to the higher binding energies, implying the stronger interaction in the g-C3N4/TiO2 interface [47]. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra of the g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05 samples are shown in Fig. 5c. The peaks located at ca. 399 eV and 400 eV correspond the C = N-C and C-N-H groups, respectively. Interestingly, compared with the pure g-C3N4, the CT and CCT0.05 samples present a positive shift of 0.2 eV in the C = N-C groups. Furthermore, the C-N-H groups in the CCT0.05 sample exhibits a positive shift of 0.4 eV comparing to the CT and g-C3N4 samples. This can be ascribed to the redistribution of electron clouds after the C dot loading [49].

Fig. 5d presents the high-resolution Ti 2p spectra of TiO2, CT and CCT0.05. It is clear that the CCT0.05 sample has a negative shift of 0.3 eV in the binding energies of Ti 2p3/2 (from 458.7 to 458.4 eV) and Ti 2p1/2 (from 464.5 to 464.2 eV) in comparison with the TiO2 and CT samples. This phenomenon can be explained by the strong interaction between host ion Ti4+ and C dots, likely involving electron donating from C dots to Ti4+ [50]. Fig. 5e shows the high-resolution O 1s spectra of TiO2, CT and CCT0.05. The binding energies located at 531.9 eV correspond to the O-H bonds. In the meantime, compared with the pure TiO2, the binding energies of Ti-O bonds for the CT and CCT0.05 samples have positive shifts of 0.1 eV and 0.2 eV, respectively. The above XPS results further confirm that there are stronger interfacial effects among TiO2, g-C3N4 and C dots in the CCT0.05 sample, implying the formation of heterostructure among these three components [14].

The optical properties of the pure C dots, TiO2, g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05 samples are recorded by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS), as given in Fig. 6 a. The C dots show much higher absorbance than other materials with a high plateau (>1.5) over the full wavelength range of 200–800 nm. In addition, it shows an raised edge below 362 nm and a narrow peak at 255 nm which is ascribed to the π–π* transition of nanocarbon [51]. The pure TiO2 has a steep absorption edge at ca. 380 nm and weaker absorption in the visible light region (400–800 nm) owing to its wide band gap [52]. The bare g-C3N4 sample shows a broader photo-absorption spectrum extending from the UV region into the visible range and holds an onset of absorption at around 460 nm [53]. The absorption spectrum of the binary g-C3N4/TiO2 composite combines the features of the two individual components, which enhances the visible- light absorption in comparison with the pure TiO2 [54]. After incorporating the C dots, the ternary CCT0.05 composite exhibits a high absorption (>0.5) below the absorption edge of the binary g-C3N4/TiO2 composite, forming a long plateau in the whole visible range. This is clearly provided by the C dots due to its highly efficient absorption in the full range of 200–800 nm [55,56].

The Kubelka–Munk transformed DRS of the various samples are depicted according to our previous work [36], as shown in Fig. S5 in the Supplementary Information. The band gaps Eg for the TiO2, g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05 are estimated to be 3.25 eV, 2.80 eV, 2.91 eV and 2.70 eV, respectively. The reduced Eg value of the ternary CCT0.05 composite may be ascribed to sensitization by C dots after deposition on the surface of the TiO2 and g-C3N4. Besides, according to Mulliken electronegativity theory reported in literatures [36], the potentials of VB maximum and CB minimum of TiO2 are further determined to be ca. 2.93 V and −0.32 V, taking the Normal Hydrogen Electrode (NHE) as reference, while those of g-C3N4 are calculated to be 1.63 V (VB) and −1.17 V (CB) relative to NHE, respectively.

To determine the electronic potentials (such as the CB minimum position, E CB) of g-C3N4 and TiO2 samples, the Mott-Schottky (MS) method was employed (Fig. 6b). Both g-C3N4 and TiO2 photoelectrodes exhibited positive slopes in Fig. 6b, indicating that they are n-type semiconductors. The flat band potential (E FB) of g-C3N4 and TiO2 can be calculated from the intercept of the tangent lines of MS curves on the x-axis, which give E FB, g-C3N4 = −1.799 V and E FB, TiO2 = −0.939 V, respectively, versus the Ag/AgCl(sat’d) reference electrode in the electrolyte with pH = 7. According to Equation (1) below [57], the E CB value at pH = 7 can be calculated from the measured E FB (at pH = 7) and converted to the value, referencing the normal hydrogen electrode potential (NHE):

Since the flat band potential EFB (and thus ECB) shifts negatively as the experimental pH value is increased (by −59 mV per pH unit) [58], it can be converted into the standard conditions (pH = 0) using Equation (2) for comparison with redox potentials in later sections [59]:

Thus the CB potentials of g-C3N4 and TiO2 are estimated to −1.189 V and −0.329 V (vs. NHE, pH = 0), respectively. Meanwhile, according to bandgap derived from the DRS results in Fig. S5 (E g = 2.80 eV for g-C3N4 and E g = 2.91 eV for C/N doped TiO2), the VB potentials E VB of g-C3N4 and C/N co-doped TiO2 are determined to be 1.611 V and 2.581 V (vs. NHE), respectively. Therefore, the experimentally measured CB potentials (vs. NHE) of −0.329 V for TiO2 (at pH = 0) and −1.189 V for g-C3N4 (pH = 0) are almost identical to those (vs. NHE) of −0.32 V for TiO2 and −1.17 V for g-C3N4 obtained by Mulliken electronegativity theory.

The PL analysis is a useful technique to study the influence of the introduced carbon dots on the recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes in various samples. In general, a stronger PL emission intensity corresponds to a faster recombination rate of the photo-induced charge carriers, leading to the lower photocatalytic performance. Fig. 6c presents the PL spectra of the pure g-C3N4, g-C3N4/TiO2 (CT) and CCT0.05 samples under 370 nm light excitation. It can be clearly seen that the pure g-C3N4 has the highest emission peak at ∼460 nm, resulting from the the band-band PL phenomenon [60]. This implies that the recombination rate of photoexcited e− and h+ is the highest. No obvious emission spectrum in the 400–600 nm region is detected for TiO2 (not shown here), consistent with the reported literatures [61]. It is noteworthy that the binary CT composite has a lower PL emission intensity as compared to pure g-C3N4, suggesting that the g-C3N4/TiO2 heterojunction restrains the recombination of photo-generated e− -h+ pairs [62]. To our surprise, the CCT0.05 sample has the lowest PL emission intensity. It can be ascribed to the possibility that C dots serve as electron mediator in the g-C3N4/TiO2 interface, forming an all-solid-state Z-scheme heterostructure, which promotes the transportation and separation of photo-induced carriers [63]. As a result, it is predicted that the CCT0.05 sample may possess the superior performance for photocatalytic H2 evolution. The transfer behavior of the photo-induced charge carriers is further illustrated by the transient photoluminescence spectra (TRPL) of the g-C3N4, CT and CCT0.05 excited with a 375 nm laser, as shown in Fig. 6d. The signals are well fitted with a tri-decayed exponentially function from the above samples, and the lifetimes are summarized in the Table 2 . The pure g-C3N4 exhibits the longer average lifetime of 10.4 ns. However, after TiO2 coupled with g-C3N4, the binary CT composite has an obviously lower fluorescent lifetime (9.1 ns). Further observation indicates that, when C dots are anchored at TiO2/g-C3N4 interface, the ternary CCT0.05 sample presents the shortest average life time of 8.28 ns. The decrease of the fluorescent lifetime may be ascribed to the formation of an additional non-radiative decay channel in favor of the separation of photoexcited charge carriers thus quenching the steady PL intensity [64]. The above results are in concurrence with the previous PL analysis.

To investigate the impact of C dots on the photocatalytic activity, the rate of H2 evolution of the as-obtained samples were measured under visible light irradiation with four LED lamps (420 nm, 3 W) using triethanolamine as a sacrificial agent. The control experiments indicate that H2 evolution was not detected in the absence of either the catalyst or light irradiation (not shown here). This implies that water splitting was purely resulted from photocatalytic processes. Fig. 7 a presents the H2 evolution rate (HER) of various catalysts. Obviously, the pure TiO2 has the lowest HER of 88 μmol h− 1g−1 due to its wide band gap of 3.25 eV, leading to very weak absorption of visible light. Although the pristine g-C3N4 possesses a narrower band gap of 2.80 eV, it exhibits only a limited HER of 278 μmol h− 1g−1. This may be ascribed to the intrinsic fast recombination rate of photo-induced e− and h+ pairs in the pristine g-C3N4 [65]. Interestingly, after g-C3N4 coupled with TiO2, the CT sample shows an increase in HER to 350 μmol h− 1g−1, which is consistent with the previous reported results [32]. Surprisingly, after loading only 0.01 g C dots, the CCT0.01 sample exhibits an obviously boosted HER of 430 μmol h− 1g−1. Further observation indicates that photocatalytic performance of the ternary CCTx composites vastly depend on the dosage of C dots. The HER gradually increases as the loading of C dots is increased from 0.01 to 0.05 g. The CCT0.05 sample displays the highest HER of 580 μmol h− 1g−1. This can be ascribed to three reasons. Firstly, the CCT0.05 powder possesses the higher specific surface area (SSA) of 52.9 m2 g−1, resulting in more active sites available for H2 production. Secondly, the CCT0.05 powder has stronger visible light absorption, leading to generation of more e− -h+ pairs in the photocatalytic process. Thirdly, it is most important that an effective heterojunction of all-solid-state Z-scheme is formed using C dots as the solid electron/hole mediator in the CCT0.05 sample (see mechanism diagram to be discussed in Fig. 9). However, although the CCTx samples have an even higher SSA as the dosage of C dots increases from 0.05 g to 0.2 g, the HER rate gradually decreases. This phenomenon can be interpreted by two factors. Firstly, when the loading of C dots exceeds a certain value, it competes with g-C3N4 and TiO2, resulting in a decrease of photogenerated e− and h+ pairs. Secondly, the excess C dots decrease the quantities of efficient heterointerfaces in the ternary CCTx samples. The comparisons of the H2 production rate for representative photocatalysts are added in page 6 of the revised supporting information in Table S1 [32,42,66,67].

Besides the photocatalytic performance, the stability of the catalysts is also an important factor to consider. Fig. 7b presents the tests of CCT0.05 for photocatalytic H2 generation three repeating runs under the identical conditions. The HER of CCT0.05 only shows a small decrease in these experiments, which may be mostly attributed to the decrease in the concentration of triethanolamine aqueous solution (as the sacrificial agent) in the photocatalytic reaction. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 7c), SEM images (Fig. S6) and the XRD patterns (Fig. S7) show that there are not obvious changes in chemical structure of CCT0.05 after three cycles [68]. As a result, it is deduced that the CCT0.05 possesses a good stability in photocatalytic processes for H2 evolution.

The transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) are further employed to explore the separation and transfer efficiency of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs. As presented in Fig. 8 a, the catalysts response very fast and reaches the equilibrium in seconds when the light is switched on and off. Among all the catalysts, pure TiO2 and g-C3N4 samples generate the lowest photocurrent, reflecting the weak visible light absorption in pure TiO2, and fast recombination of photo-induced e− -h+ pairs in pure g-C3N4. Meanwhile, the photocurrent density of the binary CT composite is obviously higher than those of the pure TiO2 and g-C3N4. This may be due to the formation of an efficient Z-scheme heterojunction, promoting the separation of carriers as we reported in a previous report [14]. Interestingly, upon introducing C dots into ternary composite, the CCT0.05 powder exhibits a further increase in the photocurrent density. This is ascribed to the fact that C dots, as a light sensitizer, boost the visible light absorption, resulting in generation of more photo-excited carriers [32].

Fig. 8b presents the Nyquist plots of EIS of the above four samples. It is well known that smaller semicircle radius represents a faster charge transfer. Fig. 8b clearly indicates that the impedance arc radius follows the order of TiO2 > g-C3N4 > CT > CCT0.05. The CCT0.05 sample displays the smallest impedance arc radius, indicating the highest transfer efficiency of photo-induced hole-electron pairs. These results further confirm that decoration of C dots is crucial to promote the charge separation and transportation in the ternary composite of C dots/g-C3N4/TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: The above experimental results on enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production can be possibly explained by the formation of an all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction (see Fig. 9 ) which is enabled by C dots as a direct electron/hole mediator. Since all the potentials have essentially the same linear dependence on the pH value following the Nernstian equation, we only plot them in the condition with pH = 0 vs. NHE for easier comparison. The conclusion does not change in other pH values. The potentials of the CBE of g-C3N4 and C-/N- doped TiO2 were calculated from the measured flat band potential by Eqs. (1) and (2) as discussed earlier, while the bandgap values for TiO2 (E g = 3.25 eV), g-C3N4 (E g = 2.80 eV) and CT (E g = 2.91 eV) are derived from the plots of (Ahv)2 versus photon energies (hv) in Fig. S5. The bandgap of CT (E g = 2.91 eV) is smaller than TiO2 (E g = 3.25 eV) due to the interband states by C-/N- doping into TiO2, as we reported in our previous report [14]. These values define the potentials of VBE of g-C3N4 (1.611 V) and TiO2 (2.921 V) and the interband potential of C-/N- doped TiO2 (2.581 V) in Fig. 9 [50]. In the ternary system, g-C3N4 and C-/N- doped TiO2 can absorb visible light and generate excited e− -h+ pairs. Comparing to our previously reported binary g-C3N4/TiO2 system in which the Z-scheme heterojunction was produced by the shallow C-/N- doping at the TiO2 surface, C dots in the ternary system provide additional fast electron/hole pathway, which further enhances the photocatalytic rate. The main features of the heterojunction are similar to the binary g-C3N4/TiO2 system except that they are explored for hydrogen production and the reaction rate is found further enhanced by the C dots mediator. Firstly, under visible-light irradiation, the photo-generated electrons in the CB (−0.329 V vs. NHE) of C-/N-doping TiO2 are transferred to C dots and then to the g-C3N4 VB (+1.611 V vs. NHE) through C dots, subsequently recombined with the photo-excited holes in g-C3N4. Meanwhile, the photo-excited electrons in CB (−1.189 V versus NHE) of g-C3N4 and holes in VB (+2.589 V vs. NHE) of the C-/N-doped TiO2 with higher redox capacity were maintained and spatially separated. Secondly, since the CB minimum (−1.189 V versus NHE) of g-C3N4 is more negative against the redox potential of H+/H2 (0 V versus NHE) at pH = 0, H+ (and H2O) can be efficiently reduced into H2 by taking the photo-induced electrons in the CB of g-C3N4. Thirdly, the holes left in the TiO2 VB can be easily consumed by sacrificial agent triethanilamine. These three factors cooperatively contribute to the efficient separation of photoinduced h+/e− pairs, resulting in the enhanced HER rate.

To verify the above mechanism, we carried out a series experiments to capture the
OH radicals that can be generated from the solution with OH− ion by the photo-excited holes if the VB edge or the interband states (in C-/N- doped TiO2) is low enough. It is well known that
OH can react with terephthalic acid to produce 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (TAOH) which gives a strong PL emission. Fig. 10 a presents the time-dependent PL spectra of the aqueous solution with 2 mM NaOH and 0.5 mM terephthalic acid in the presence of both CCT0.05 sample and four 3 W 420 nm LED lights. Obviously, we found that PL intensity of the hydroxyl terephthalic acid gradually increases versus time. This implies that the
OH radical was indeed generated on the surface of the CCT0.05 sample. If the H2 production process of ternary CCT0.05 catalyst abides a Type II heterojunction scheme, the excited electrons in the g-C3N4 CB (−1.189 V) would transfer to the conduction band of TiO2 (−0.329 V). At the meantime, the holes left in the interband states (+2.581 V) of C/N-dopants TiO2 will transfer to the g-C3N4 VB (+1.611 V). In this case, the holes in the g-C3N4 VB (1.611 V) can hardly oxidize OH− into
OH radical due to the almost same potential as the redox potential of OH− /
OH (1.99 V at pH = 0) [69]. Therefore, the type II heterojunction cannot explain our experiment results of the
OH radical production on the surface of the CCT0.05 sample under the 420 nm visible light.

Fig. 10b clearly shows that both the binary CT catalyst and the ternary CCT0.05 catalyst generate steadily increasing TAOH PL intensity over time when exposed to visible light (420 nm) while the pure TiO2 and pure g-C3N4 have negligible TAOH PL emission. Further observation indicates that the ternary CCT0.05 catalyst generates the highest TAOH PL intensity. This can only be explained by the all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism illustrated in Fig. 9, where the holes remain in the interband states in TiO2 at a potential of 2.581 V which is more positive than the potential of OH− /
OH (1.99 V at pH = 0). Thus they can easily oxidize OH− into
OH. These results show that C dots in the ternary CCT0.05 catalyst serve as an electron conductor, which can enhance the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers and further promote the photocatalytic rate. This forms an all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction, which is consistent with literature [17].

A ternary composite photocatalyst consisting of TiO2, g-C3N4 and C dots has been prepared by impregnation precipitation methods. The materials have been systematically characterized with SEM, TEM, BET surface analyses, XRD, XPS, FTIR and UV–vis absorption measurements in comparison with the pure components and the binary g-C3N4/TiO2 system. At the proper C dots loading, the ternary photocatalyst presents 66% higher hydrogen production rate than the binary g-C3N4/TiO2 photocatalyst in water splitting by visible light. The improved photocatalytic activity of the ternary catalyst can be assigned to the formation of a stable all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction. The mechanism has been verified by the generation of
OH radicals by the holes in the interband states of C-/N- dopants. These results provide new approaches to optimizing heterojunction photocatalysts.
